coredata.dc:title: Design and control of the hybrid lithium-ion/lead–acid battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: 2022 The 3rd International Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Wodyk, Sebastian
Wieczorek, Maciej
Witaszek, Paweł
Poliszkiewicz, Rafał
coredata.dc:description: This paper describes method of design and control of a hybrid battery built with lead–acid and lithium-ion batteries. In the proposed hybrid, bidirectional interleaved DC/DC converter is integrated with lithium-ion battery, and is an interface for lead–acid battery. Control system allows uninterrupted operation of the hybrids even in the case of a sudden shut down of the lithium-ion battery. Thanks to the proposed reference voltage selection method, system operates stable in a parallel connection. Experimental tests were carried out using prototypes of the proposed batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid battery
Parallel operation
Lead-acid battery
Lithium-ion battery

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 2022 The 3rd International Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ICPEE 2022) 29–31 December, Singapore

head.ce:title: Design and control of the hybrid lithium-ion/lead–acid battery

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Control and Industrial Electronics, Faculty of Electrical Engineering, Warsaw University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper describes method of design and control of a hybrid battery built with lead–acid and lithium-ion batteries. In the proposed hybrid, bidirectional interleaved DC/DC converter is integrated with lithium-ion battery, and is an interface for lead–acid battery. Control system allows uninterrupted operation of the hybrids even in the case of a sudden shut down of the lithium-ion battery. Thanks to the proposed reference voltage selection method, system operates stable in a parallel connection. Experimental tests were carried out using prototypes of the proposed batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Effective use of renewable energy sources, like photovoltaics (PV) or wind, requires energy storage due to the stochastic nature of the sources and the demands. While it is intuitive that off-grid systems cannot operate without electrical energy storage, its use seems to be obligatory also in on-grid systems in near future, because of challenges facing the power grid, like growing share of unpredictable renewable energy sources, in power generation, and rapid development of electric vehicles charging infrastructure on the other hand.

Among many technologies that allows for storing energy, electrochemical batteries are most popular in residential PV installations. Lead–acid batteries are popular mainly because of low cost and high reliability [1], what makes them attractive, especially in the developing countries. However, they feature short life-cycle and are not resistant to conditions that may appear in PV systems like undercharging, low state of charge (SoC), high charging current [2]. Therefore lithium-ion batteries are usually proposed as an alternative, nevertheless, due to the higher cost, they are used mostly in developed countries, where PV system operates in on-grid mode, and battery is used for the purpose of an energy balancing [1,3]. The combination of these two types of batteries into a hybrid storage leads to a significant reduction of phenomena unfavorable for lead–acid battery and lower the cost of the storage compared to lithium-ion batteries.

Hybrid energy storage, that combines two types of batteries, can be made with direct connection between them, forming one DC-bus [4], nevertheless such a connection eliminates possibility of an active energy management and power distribution between batteries, what is necessary to reduce lead–acid battery degradation. Thus, more popular approach is use of power electronic converter between batteries, that allows for current control. In [5] authors proposed plug-in module, consisting of lithium-ion battery and supercapacitor, that is connected to the lead–acid battery energy storage via bidirectional DC/DC converters. The aim of the module is to reduce current stress of lead–acid battery, and as a result to enhance its lifetime.

This paper presents design and control of a hybrid energy storage consisting of lead–acid (LA) battery and lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4, LFP) battery, with built-in bidirectional DC/DC converter. The article discusses issues facing construction and control of power electronic converter, specific due to integration with LiFePO4 battery, including power losses, EMC filters, current and voltage control, emergency states handling as well as parallel operation of a hybrid sets. Although hybrid connection of a different types of batteries is known in the literature, integration of the lithium-ion battery with converter into one device, with terminal to direct LA connection is novel approach. Thanks to that, several advantages are achieved like: smaller volume of the battery – converter set in relation to separate devices; utilization of the battery housing as a heat sink what mitigates consumption of materials. Moreover, proposed solution is flexible in terms of capacity expansion by parallel connection, without need for communication between batteries, even if any LFP malfunction occurs thanks to the proposed novel method of the voltage maintenance.
Both simulation and experimental results are provided. Experimental tests were carried out using a prototype of proposed hybrid battery.

In order to ensure controllability of the hybrid battery, power electronic converter needs to operate in whole voltage characteristic of both LA and LFP batteries. Therefore, converter topology should be determined taking into account nominal voltage of the batteries. It is possible to maintain bidirectional power flow for equal nominal voltages of the batteries, using bidirectional buck-boost cascade converter [6], that may be also utilized in a modular construction [7]. Although for such a design it is possible to achieve the continuous current in the converters inductor, current of the voltage sources connected to both sides of the converter will be discontinuous, due to the nature of the converter, operating like traditional buck or boost depending on the needs. Therefore bulk EMC filters on both sides of the converter are necessary in order to reduce current ripples in the wires, and hence to reduce EMC emission. A conventional bidirectional interleaved converter requires bulk EMC filter only on the high side, because converter inductor current, that contains switching frequency triangular component, flows thorough the voltage source connected to the low side. Hybrid battery proposed in the paper basis on two-leg interleaved converter, presented in Fig. 1(a). LA battery is connected to the low side and its nominal voltage is 12 V, whereas LFP is connected to the high side and its nominal voltage is 25.6 V, what gives voltage ratio about 0.47. As can be seen in Fig. 1(b), two-leg configuration is the most favorable from the low side current ripples point of view, if the voltages are close to the nominal values.

Switching frequency of the converter is 50 kHz and is set arbitrary. It should be noted that pulse width modulation (PWM) frequency is limited not only by the semiconductors power losses, but also by the microcontroller capabilities, e.g. PWM resolution that depends on the processor clock frequency.

Selection of the interleaved inductor demands calculation if its maximal instantaneous value as well as root mean square (rms) value. First component is important from the inductor saturation point of view, whereas second one from the heat generation point of view. Those values can be calculated using (1) and (2).
(1)
i
I m a x

i
L 0

Δ
i
I
2
(2)
I
I r m s
=
i
L 0

i
I
2
12
where
i
I m a x
– maximal instantaneous value of the inductor current,
I
I r m s
– rms value of the inductor current,
i
L 0
– average value of the low side current, Δ i
I
– peak-to-peak value of the inductor current ripples. Since the inductor peak-to-peak value depends on the inductance, target inductor should be selected iteratively. At first step, average value of the low side current should be established. Then, maximal peak-to-peak inductor current ripples value should be calculated for selected inductance. It should be noticed that low side current ripples and inductor current ripples reach maximal values for different duty cycle, and current ripples for each inductor may be considered as a one-leg converter. Then, those value can be calculated with (3).
(3)
Δ
i
I
=
1 − D
D
T
s w

H
L
I
where D – PWM duty cycle,
T
s w
– switching period,
u
H
– high side voltage,
L
I
– inductance in each leg of the converter. As can be seen, maximal inductor current ripples occurs for duty cycle
D = 0 . 5
, and highest possible the high side voltage, which for calculation was assumed 29 V. Results of the inductor selection are presented in Table 1.

Elimination of high frequency components from batteries currents requires LC filters on both sides of the hybrid battery, however high side current filtration is more challenging in terms of capacitor selection, due to the discontinuous nature of current, and hence high amplitude ripples. Although, the switching frequency
f
s w
is 50 kHz, the main frequency of both high side and low side current ripples is doubled due to the use of the two-leg converter. As can be seen in Fig. 1(a), role of an EMC filters is played by
L
F H
C
F H
on the high side and by
L
F L
C
F L
on the low side. Parameters of a passive elements applied in filters are provided in Table 2. In general, transfer function of both high side and low side filters, understood as a ratio of the battery current to the converter current Laplace transform, is described by (4). Bode plots of the EMC filters are presented in Fig. 2(a).

where symbols are equivalent to the quantities presented in Table 2.

It can be assumed that chokes currents contains only DC components and capacitors supply current ripples, therefore chokes, as well as low side capacitor selection is trivial, on the other hand high side capacitor selection is challenging, due to rectangular nature of the current in the converter legs, as can be seen in Fig. 2(b). Instantaneous value of the high side capacitor current
i

Power losses are the key design parameter of the converter. Calculation of loss in each element allows for evaluation of their selection correctness. Particularly in terms of the transistors selection. The requirements for transistors are generally: breakdown voltage, low switching and conduction power losses. For an application discussed in this paper silicone MOSFETs (Metal-Oxide Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor) are the most beneficial in terms of performance and cost. Unfortunately, in most cases datasheets of such a devices do not contain direct information about turn-on energy
E
o n
and turn-off energy
E
o f f
, so certain calculations are necessary to evaluate switching loss. Basing on the information given in data sheet, mentioned energies can be calculated using method presented in [8]. A conduction loss is calculated basing on drain current rms value (see (7) and (8)) and MOSFET on-resistance
r
O N ,
which equals 1.42 m Ω for selected transistors.
(7)
I
Q 1 r m s
=
D
i

Δ

(8)
I
Q 2 r m s

I
I r m s
where
I
Q 1 r m s
– rms value of the top transistor current,
I
Q 2 r m s
– rms value of the bottom transistor current. Total power loss may be therefore calculated as presented in (9).
(9)
P

Specific power losses are described in Table 3, although it is necessary to calculate duty cycle D. In general duty cycle D depends on the
u
L
to
u
H
ratio, however accurate calculation demands taking current into account, then D may be approximated using (10).
(10)
D

i
L 0

=
u
L
−
R
i n L
+
R
L I
+
r
o n
i
L 0
u
H
+
R
i n H
u
L
u
H

+
r
o n
i
L 0
where
R
i n L
,
R
i n H
– low side and high side sources internal resistances respectively, including EMC filters chokes resistances, see Table 4. Calculated total power loss
P
t o t a l
for nominal voltages as a function of low side current
i
L 0
is presented in Fig. 3(a). Maximal power loss is about 12 W, for both positive and negative current. Specific power losses pie chart is presented in Fig. 3(c), as can be seen interleaved inductors have the dominant share of the total power loss, for maximal current. It should be noted that voltages of both sides of the converter are varying, what makes power losses dependent on them. Full analysis therefore requires a four-dimensional chart. Seeing that high side voltage
u
H
varies in a wider range than low side voltage
u
L
, a three-dimensional power loss graph as well as an efficiency graph have been prepared using
u
H
as an additional axis, what can be seen in Fig. 3(b) and (d). Converter features high efficiency in a wide range of current (over 98%).

Control system of the proposed hybrid battery is presented in Fig. 4. As can be seen, reference low side current may come from a different superior controllers, i.e. power distribution algorithm or high side voltage control loop. The first one is responsible for an energy management in a normal operating conditions, so it is an ordinary reference generator from the control system point of view. Optimization of the power flow in a hybrid battery is not a purpose of this paper and therefore this issue will not be discussed. The second one is high side voltage regulation that is considered as an emergency state, in case the LFP battery management system (BMS) will turn off for any case. Then, converter will provide appropriate voltage uninterruptedly. Further, for both modes, the charging and discharging of the LA battery can be defined as different cases, depending on the reference current sign.

If current is positive, additional low side voltage controller is applied to ensure constant voltage (CV) charging by decreasing reference current. If voltage is below CV level, constant current (CC) charging is performed, then the reference current is used as saturation of the controller, preventing desired value from being exceeded. Reference current is also utilized as a feedforward signal, thank to that CC charging dynamics is not deteriorated.

The final stage of the control system is current control loop, with voltages ratio as a feedforward signal. Measured values are filtered with analog RC low-pass filters (LPF) with 10 kHz cut-off frequency.

Whereas current control in case of power distribution in a hybrid battery, as well as control of the charging process are typical tasks for any hybrid battery, handling of the described emergency state is challenging when it comes to parallel connection of hybrids. Then, more than one controller is trying to regulate the same quantity that is high side voltage (
u
H
). This issue is covered in Section 4.

The prototype of the LFP battery with integrated DC/DC converter is presented in Fig. 5(a). Laboratory rig was built with two sets of hybrids consisting of 20 Ah LFP batteries and 120 Ah LA batteries, setup is presented in Fig. 5(b). The hybrids are connected each other at LFP outputs, and connected to the programable load and the laboratory power supply.

Fig. 6. presents current regulation in a single hybrid. Reference was set arbitrarily, in order to force the toughest operating conditions that is maximal possible charging and discharging current of LA battery. In this case, maximal charging current equals 20 A due to the battery capabilities, whereas maximal discharging current equals −50 A, what follows from the converter capabilities. The results are considered as satisfactory.

Within the research, a heating test was performed, in order to verify maximal temperature rise on a crucial elements of the converter, namely MOSFETS, interleaved inductors and housing, which acts like a heatsink. Test was performed for low side current
i
L
equal −50 A and nominal voltages of both sides of the converter, and 25 °C ambient temperature. Results are presented in Fig. 7. As can be seen, the hottest elements of the converter are interleaved inductors, reaching 82 °C, what confirms power losses analysis presented in Section 2.2. Temperature of the MOSFETS does not exceed 60 °C, what stands for safe operating conditions them. Temperature of the hybrid battery housing reached 46 °C.

Further experiential tests focused on parallel operation of the hybrid batteries during the emergency state. As a first case, situation in which reference high side voltage
u
H
∗
is constant and equals 25.6 V, what is nominal voltage of the LFP battery. As can be seen in Fig. 8(a), such an approach results in undesirable energy transfer between LA batteries,deepening the difference in a state of charge (SOC). It is caused by parallel operation of two voltage controllers using the same variable as a feedback, however, in practice burdened with different measuring errors, what leads to an unstable operation. An appropriate operation of parallel connected hybrids requires application of a droop control known form DC microgrids. In order to keep system simple, there is no communication between hybrids. Distributed droop control may be executed using voltage drop on a virtual resistance [9,10]. Nevertheless, some coefficients, among the others, droop resistance need to be obtained, which requires additional algorithms and thus more complex control system. Moreover, control systems known from DC microgrids are aiming for keeping constant DC voltage. The presented system is strictly a battery, from the users point of view, even if the emergency state occurs, so
u
H
should be varying depending on the SOC. Taking into account that LFP and LA batteries features similar characteristics, the proposed solution is to keep a constant voltages ratio, then reference high side voltage
u
H
∗
equals
2u
L
. Results for such an approach is presented in Fig. 8(b). As can be seen, convergence is achieved for both current and voltage. System leads to equalization of the LA voltages, or to achieve half of the LFP battery voltage if emergency occurs only in one hybrid. Fig. 9 presents comparison of a current sharing under load. As can be seen proposed control ensure stable operation unlike the constant voltage approach. Current sharing is executed naturally, just like in the systems consisting of parallel connected batteries.

The article presents step-by-step design method of a hybrid battery consisting of LA and LFP batteries. In the proposed hybrid storage, DC/DC converter is integrated with LFP battery, so it can be considered as a plug-in solution. Essential issues were discussed like converter topology, passive elements selection including EMC filters, power losses and control system.

Selection of the passive elements as well as the transistors was carried out basing on a power losses calculation for each element. Thanks to that, it is possible to determine efficiency of the converter, and the impact of a specific element on it. Moreover, the article presents charts of the theoretical power losses and efficiency.

Based on the presented considerations, a prototype of a hybrid battery was constructed, which was used in the experiment. Within experimental tests parallel operation of hybrids were discussed what is particularly important in an emergency states. As a single battery is intended to be part of a larger energy storage, parallel operation is considered a natural work condition. Then, power sharing is achieved naturally, however for an emergency states, understood as LFP malfunction, energy sharing depends on the voltage control strategy. The proposed control method ensures stable operation and current sharing such as in parallel connected electrochemical batteries, however with assumed current limitation.
Further work will be focused on the series connection of the modules, including building of a high voltage storages.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Maciej Wieczorek reports financial support was provided by National Centre for Research and Development. Sebastian Wodyk and Maciej Wieczorek have patent #PCT/PL2022/050077 pending to Breeze Energies sp. z o. o.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by The National Centre for Research and Development (Poland) within the Project under Grant POIR.01.01.01-00-1439/19.7/03942.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy storage for mitigating the variability of renewable electricity sources: An updated review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Beaudin, H. Zareipour, A. Schellenberglabe, and W. Rosehart, Energy storage for mitigating the variability of renewable electricity sources: An updated review, Energy Sustain. Dev., 14 (4) (2010) 302–314.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aging mechanisms and service life of lead–acid batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Ruetschi, 2004, Aging mechanisms and service life of lead–acid batteries, in Journal of Power Sources, 127 (1–2) 33–44.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A life cycle-cost analysis of Li-ion and lead-acid BESSs and their actively hybridized ESSs with supercapacitors for islanded microgrid applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Torkashvand, A. Khodadadi, M. B. Sanjareh, and M. H. Nazary, A Life Cycle-Cost Analysis of Li-ion and Lead-Acid BESSs and Their Actively Hybridized ESSs with Supercapacitors for Islanded Microgrid Applications, IEEE Access, 8 (2020) 153215–153225.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance of a hybrid battery energy storage system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Dascalu, S. Sharkh, A. Cruden, and P. Stevenson, Performance of a hybrid battery energy storage system, Energy Reports, 8 (2022) 1–7.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery lifetime enhancement via smart hybrid energy storage plug-in module in standalone photovoltaic power system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Jing, C. H. Lai, D. K. X. Ling, W. S. H. Wong, and M. L. D. Wong, Battery lifetime enhancement via smart hybrid energy storage plug-in module in standalone photovoltaic power system, J. Energy Storage, 21 (2018) (2019) 586–598.

Solero, Study of bi-directional buck-boost converter topologies for application in electrical vehicle motor drives, in APEC ’98 Thirteenth Annual Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition, 1998, 287–293.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid energy storage system with active power-mix control in a dual-chemistry battery pack for light electric vehicles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Graovac, M. Pürschel, and K. Andreas, MOSFET Power Losses Calculation Using the Data- Sheet Parameters, Infineon Technol. AG, 0 (July) (2006) 1–23.
